summary: Gruber investigates Apple's removal of Airfoil Speakers Touch from the App Store, concluding it's less capricious than it first appeared. The issue centers on version 3's new feature that turned iOS devices into AirPlay receivers by reverse-engineering Apple's protocol. While Rogue Amoeba used only public APIs, Apple interprets its rules broadly enough to prohibit using public APIs to do things Apple doesn't want done. Gruber argues Apple likely considers 'non-public API' to encompass all non-public Apple technologies, not just literal programming interfaces. He notes this reflects the App Store's implicit rule zero: Apple won't publish apps they don't want, regardless of technical compliance. He also debunks speculation that the removal was related to a similar feature coming in iOS 6.
key_points: Apple removed Airfoil Speakers Touch v3 specifically because its new feature turned iOS devices into AirPlay receivers by reverse-engineering Apple's protocol, not because of the app's three-year presence in the store

The App Store rule about using APIs 'in the manner prescribed by Apple' effectively means developers cannot use public APIs to do things Apple doesn't want, which is simultaneously unsurprising and a little crazy
If Apple has a problem with the potential uses of a documented public API, that's an indication something is wrong with the API itself
The spirit of App Store rules matters more than the letter, but the fundamental problem for developers is that nobody outside Apple knows what the 'spirit' actually is
Gruber's sources confirm the removal had nothing to do with any potential iOS 6 features — Apple doesn't preemptively remove apps it plans to compete with
money_quotes: As I read it, rule 3.3.1 effectively means 'You may not use private APIs, and you may not use public APIs to do things we don't want you to do.'
That's simultaneously unsurprising, and, a little crazy.
If Apple has a problem with the potential uses of a documented public API, is that not an indication that there's something wrong with the API?
The implicit rule 0 of the App Store has always been that Apple isn't going to publish an app they don't want in the store, and that's that.
It's not enough to comply with the letter of the rules; developers must comply with the spirit of them as well.
That's the heart of the problem for third-party developers: nobody who didn't write the rules knows what the 'spirit' of the rules is.

The app may not be using a 'non-public API', but it is decrypting a non-public streaming audio format, and Apple perhaps considers 'non-public API' to cover all 'non-public Apple technologies', not merely literal application programming interfaces.
key_insight: Apple's App Store rules effectively prohibit not just private API usage but any use of public APIs to achieve outcomes Apple hasn't sanctioned, making the 'spirit' of the rules more important than the letter — yet only Apple knows what that spirit is.
